slides.heading: Meet Kavya 👩‍💻
slides.body:
Kavya is learning to code. She's been at it for a few weeks and just finished something she's really proud of — a simple to-do app.

It works. Users can add tasks, check them off, delete them. It's not perfect, but it's *hers*.

She saved it as one file on her laptop.

Then she decided to add something new.
bullets: 💻 One laptop, one project folder
📄 One file: todo.js
✅ Everything working well
🤔 "I want to add a dark mode..."

slides.heading: Disaster #1 — The working version is gone 😱
slides.body:
Kavya spent two hours adding dark mode. She edited parts of her code she'd never touched before.

Then she saved and opened the app.

*Nothing worked.*

The font rendering was broken. The checkboxes disappeared. Even features she didn't touch were broken.

She pressed Ctrl+Z about 40 times. The editor undid everything — including the parts that were working before.

She sat staring at a blank file.

"If only I had saved a copy before I started..."
bullets: ❌ 2 hours of work, gone
❌ The working version she had before — overwritten
❌ Ctrl+Z went too far back
😔 Starting from memory isn't the same as starting from the original

slides.heading: Kavya gets smart — she starts making copies
slides.body:
After that painful experience, Kavya makes a rule: "Before I change anything, I'll make a copy."

So she starts saving copies of her file.

At first, it feels great. A safety net!

But then a week passes...

Tap each file below to see what Kavya's project folder looks like now.
bullets: 📁 She was careful, organized, responsible
📂 But her folder slowly turned into... a nightmare
🤔 Can you guess which file is the real one?
👇 Tap the files to find out

slides.heading: Disaster #2 — The folder of doom 🗂️
slides.body:
Two weeks later, Kavya needs to go back to the version that had working animations.

She has 7 files. None of them are named clearly enough to know what's in them.

She opens each one. She can't tell which has the animations because the code looks almost identical.

She spends 45 minutes opening, comparing, getting confused.

"There must be a better way to keep track of what changed in each version..."
bullets: ❌ 7 copies of the same project
❌ No clear way to know what's in each one
❌ 45 minutes wasted just *finding* the right version
😩 What was changed? When? Why? No one knows.

slides.heading: Disaster #3 — Working with someone else 🤯
slides.body:
Kavya's friend Rohan wants to help. Great!

They split the work. Kavya works on the search feature. Rohan works on filters.

They work for 3 hours separately.

Rohan emails Kavya his file. She copies it into the project.

Her search feature? **Gone.** His file replaced everything she wrote.

Neither of them had done anything wrong. They just had no way to work on the same project safely.

That's the third and hardest problem.
bullets: 👩‍💻 Kavya: 3 hours on search feature
👦 Rohan: 3 hours on filters
📧 Rohan sends a file. Kavya pastes it in.
💥 Kavya's 3 hours of work: gone.

slides.heading: Three real problems — every developer faces these
slides.body:
Kavya isn't alone. Every developer who has ever worked without version control has hit these exact same walls.

They're not made-up problems. They happen every day to people learning to code.

But here's the good news:

**All three of these problems have elegant solutions.** And in the next steps, we're going to discover those solutions — one by one — before we even open a terminal.

Tap each problem to refresh your memory.
bullets: Problem 1: Broke working code, can't go back
Problem 2: Too many copies, can't find the right one
Problem 3: Two people working together — overwriting each other
